questions.scenario: The on-call operator needs a count of ERROR lines in /home/student/logs/app.log.
questions.question: Count the number of lines containing 'ERROR' in /home/student/logs/app.log.
questions.description: grep -c counts matching lines (not occurrences — if a line has two ERRORs, it counts as 1). grep -v inverts the match. grep -i is case-insensitive. grep -E enables extended regex.
use_cases: Alert if error count exceeds threshold
Summarise log quality at a glance

questions.scenario: You need to extract just the usernames from /home/student/data/users.csv (the first column).
questions.question: Use awk to print only the first column from /home/student/data/users.csv, skipping the header.
questions.description: awk '{print $1}' prints the first field. -F sets the delimiter. NR is the current line number. NR>1 skips the header. awk is a full programming language — the most powerful text column processor in the shell.

questions.scenario: A log file has 'ERROR' entries but the new format requires 'CRITICAL'. Fix it with sed.
questions.question: Use sed to replace all occurrences of 'ERROR' with 'CRITICAL' in /home/student/logs/app.log and print the result.
questions.description: sed 's/old/new/g' substitutes all occurrences on each line (g = global). Without g, only the first match per line. sed -i edits in place. Always cp file.bak first before using -i in production.
use_cases: Fix typos or terminology changes in config files
Remove or replace specific strings in files

questions.scenario: You need the role column (3rd field) from each row of /home/student/data/users.csv.
questions.question: Use cut to extract the third field from /home/student/data/users.csv using comma as delimiter.
questions.description: cut -d',' -f3 extracts field 3, delimited by comma. -f1,3 extracts fields 1 and 3. -f2- extracts from field 2 to end. cut is simpler than awk for single-field extraction from delimited files.
use_cases: Extract a specific column from CSV or TSV
Trim field lists from structured output

questions.scenario: You need to convert all lowercase letters in a string to uppercase using tr.
questions.question: Use tr to convert 'reactor block alpha' to uppercase.
questions.description: tr translates characters. tr 'a-z' 'A-Z' maps each lowercase letter to its uppercase equivalent. tr -d deletes characters. tr -s squeezes repeated characters. Simpler than awk or sed for character-level transformations.
use_cases: Convert file content to consistent case
Remove specific characters (tr -d '\r' to strip Windows line endings)
hints: Feed the string via echo or here-string

questions.scenario: You need a frequency breakdown of which log levels appear most in /home/student/logs/app.log.
questions.question: Use the frequency idiom (sort | uniq -c | sort -rn) to rank log levels by occurrence.
questions.description: The frequency idiom: grep to extract, sort to group, uniq -c to count consecutive duplicates, sort -rn to rank by count descending. You'll use this combination almost daily as a sysadmin.
use_cases: Find most common errors in a log
Rank most active users in access logs
Identify most frequent commands in history
hints: grep -oE extracts only the matched text
